museum.name: Gaming Time Machine
museum.dek: Pac-Man receives its full Japanese release, turning the arcade away from pure shooting and toward character, maze rhythm and personality.
museum.curatorNote: July is Pac-Man's full-release drawer, after May's location-test anchor.

sections.title: News
sections.subtitle: Five researched moments from an early videogame year, with broad context separated from confident month-level claims.

items.title: Pac-Man is released widely in Japan
items.body: After May location testing, Namco's maze game receives its broader Japanese release.
items.sourceNote: Pac-Man sources cite July 1980 full release.

items.title: Arcade design gets a mascot-shaped future
items.body: Pac-Man makes a game character feel like a brand before the word mascot dominates the medium.

items.title: The maze becomes social memory
items.body: Patterns, fruit and ghost movement become part of arcade conversation.

items.title: The UK arcade will absorb the character wave unevenly
items.body: Pac-Man's global cultural weight grows through cabinets arriving locally, not a single UK launch moment.

items.title: Shooter dominance is no longer guaranteed
items.body: The year's arcade shelf now has room for personality, anxiety, tanks, robots and mazes.

sections.title: Releases
sections.subtitle: A deliberately sparse shelf: only releases supported by the sources reviewed are displayed.

items.title: Pac-Man / Puck Man
items.body: Namco's maze character game begins in Japan through May location testing and reaches wider Japanese release in July.
items.certainty: full Japanese release after May location test
items.sourceNote: Pac-Man sources cite May 22 location test and July 1980 full Japanese release.

sections.title: Hardware
sections.subtitle: Four objects explaining the month: public cabinets, home consoles, microcomputers and control technology.

items.title: Atari VCS as home arcade
items.body: By July 1980, Atari's console can plausibly sell itself as a way to bring a famous cabinet home.
items.sourceNote: Atari VCS Space Invaders and Adventure sources.

items.title: Namco Pac-Man arcade board
items.body: Pac-Man makes a maze, character and soundscape into one of the decade's most durable objects.

items.title: Apple II adventure platform
items.body: Mystery House shows the Apple II as a storytelling and graphics machine, not just a hobby computer.

items.title: Vector arcade cabinets
items.body: Battlezone's wireframe view points toward first-person spectacle in the arcade.

sections.title: Magazine Covers
sections.subtitle: Paper remains the map: computing, electronics and arcade context before games media fully settles.

sections.title: Online Life
sections.subtitle: Networked play exists at the edges, but ordinary players live in a world of paper, cabinets and local knowledge.

items.title: Online life was not the ordinary visitor experience
items.body: For most players in July 1980, games are discovered through arcades, shops, magazines, clubs and word of mouth rather than online services.
items.sourceNote: Included to avoid projecting later online habits backward.

items.title: Institutional networks remain the edge case
items.body: PLATO, university systems and early networked computing matter historically, but they are not normal UK consumer gaming.
items.sourceNote: PLATO and early networked game context.

items.title: Paper is still the search engine
items.body: Magazines, adverts, catalogues, manuals and club newsletters explain what machines and games exist.
items.sourceNote: BYTE, Creative Computing, PCW and electronics-magazine context.

items.title: The arcade is the live feed
items.body: High scores, queues and overheard advice make the cabinet a public information system.

sections.title: What It Felt Like
sections.subtitle: A young medium, a patchy record, and a future split between public machines and home hardware.

items.title: The future was still public
items.body: The arcade feels crowded with futures: yellow characters, missile trails, wireframe tanks, talking robots and old invaders still earning coins.

items.title: The home shelf was changing shape
items.body: The living room is no longer just Pong. Cartridges can now promise arcade names, secret worlds and a shelf that grows.

items.title: Britain saw the edges first
items.body: Britain is on the edge of the home-micro era, but the arcade still feels like the brightest room in gaming.

items.title: The record is still uneven
items.body: Exact dates are often regional, trade-led or retrospective. The exhibit keeps month-level certainty separate from year-level context.
